case_name: Afridi v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia
text:
2025 BCSC 2296 Afridi v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA
Citation:
Afridi v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia,
2025 BCSC 2296
Date: 20251030
Docket: S228888
Registry: New Westminster
Between:
Qasim Hameed Afridi
Plaintiff
And
College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia
and Northern Health Authority
Defendants
Before: The Honourable Justice Crerar
Oral Reasons for Judgment
Counsel for the Plaintiff:
V. Sidhu
Counsel for the Defendant, College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia:
A.K. Harlingten
Counsel for the Defendant, Northern Health Authority:
J. Abrioux
Place and Date of Trial/Hearing:
Abbotsford, B.C.
October 29-30, 2025
Place and Date of Judgment:
Abbotsford, B.C.
October 30, 2025
A. INTRODUCTION
[1] THE COURT: These are my oral reasons for judgment in this matter. I make the usual reservation to edit or expand these reasons if a transcript is ordered or for any other reason. The result will not, of course, change. In the course of these oral reasons, I will make reference to statutes and case law. In the interests of time, I will not read out full citations for those references. I will also be quoting from statutes and the Rules and case law and other materials. Again, in the interests of time, I am not going to read those out in court. However, those citations and quotations in their entirety will be incorporated in my reasons for judgment.
[2] The plaintiff and the defendants (the "College"; the "Health Authority" or "NHA") bring mirror cross-applications. Under Rule 6‑1, the plaintiff seeks leave to file his second amended notice of civil claim. Under Rules 9-5(1)(a) ("discloses no reasonable claim"); (b) ("unnecessary, scandalous, frivolous or vexatious"); and (d) ("otherwise an abuse of the process of the court"); the defendants apply to strike the existing claim, without leave to amend.
[3] It is the second time that these mirror applications have been heard. On May 29, 2025, Justice Caldwell denied the plaintiff leave to amend. In unpublished reasons indexed as 2025 BCSC 983, Caldwell J noted the myriad deficiencies in the existing and the proposed pleadings before him:
[8] The pleadings are unfocussed, rambling, prolix, and, in some particulars, they refer to claims not properly founded in or known to law in BC. The pleadings fail entirely to properly identify the details or particulars of the claims regarding defamation, including the identities of the persons alleged to have uttered the impugned statements. They fail to identify the basis of an employment relationship to serve as a foundation for a wrongful dismissal claim. This is by no means an exhaustive list of the defects in the existing claim and the proposed amended claim.
. . .
[11] I have reviewed the proposed amendments to the pleadings several times. If anything, they get worse and less clear as they progress through the steps of amendment. The amended claims are fraught with problems. Therefore, the application to amend the claim to the form attached to the application is dismissed.
[4] That said, Caldwell J concluded that he was "unable to determine that all [of the plaintiff's] claims, if properly framed, pled, and supported by evidence, are entirely without merit": para 13. Accordingly, he expressly provided "the plaintiff a 'last chance' to amend his claim to an acceptable form which properly identifies his claims and abides the Rules and law relating to pleadings": para 14.
[5] Justice Caldwell ordered the plaintiff to pay the defendants their costs of the application in any event of the cause, payable forthwith: para 15.
[6] I will turn to the background here and I will start with the plaintiff's own background.
B. BACKGROUND
1. Dr Afridi
[7] I adopt Caldwell J's succinct description of the background facts leading to the litigation:
[3] The plaintiff is a medical doctor with a specialty in psychiatry. He obtained his training in Pakistan and Ireland. He immigrated to BC in 2016, settled in Fort St. John, and began practicing as a Provisionally Licenced Psychiatrist.
[4] The plaintiff required supervision during the provisional period. The defendant BC Northern Health Authority ("BCNHA") has jurisdiction to recommend supervisors for this purpose.
[5] The plaintiff came into conflict with his supervisors and other medical practitioners in the area. In addition, medical practitioners and patients made several complaints regarding the plaintiff to the defendant College of Physicians and Surgeons (the "College"), which regulates the practice of medicine in BC.
[8] In 2018 and other times, the plaintiff filed formal complaints against his supervisors and colleagues. These communications were particularly acrimonious, as summarised in the Health Authority submission, which the Court adopts:
13. During the breakdown of the Plaintiff's relationship with Dr. J[1], he had indicated an intention to resign from NHA, which he later reversed in February, 2018. On or about February 26, 2018, NHA wrote to the Plaintiff about re-joining the medical staff at the Hospital. This was contingent on certain conditions, including that the Plaintiff obtain and maintain licensure from the College and that the Plaintiff qualify for and maintain membership on the Medical Staff of NHA with clinical privileges at the Hospital. The Plaintiff accepted NHA's offer to re-join the medical staff at the Hospital.
14. The Plaintiff continued to inappropriately respond to any negative feedback delivered by Dr. K and Dr. T. On or about May 18, 2018, Dr. K met with the Plaintiff in his role as supervisor to discuss with the Plaintiff certain issues that had been brought to his attention. The Plaintiff responded angrily and made comments of a threatening nature, including but not limited to:
(a) that he was "going to resign in his position at Northern Health if his supervision report was not favourable", and that he was going to "take people down with me" and that "if you are dying you take your enemies with you";
(b) that he was going to "spit on the floor in the front of everyone to show his disgust";
(c) that he "was going to make media and news reports" and that this was his "way of hurting some of the people back who have upset him";
(d) that he was going to "retaliate" against the "heartless devils" with whom he worked;
(e) that Dr. K had "tarnished his reputation and you can expect retaliation";
(f) that he was going to "counter-accuse people [with whom he worked] of taking drugs";
(g) threats to Dr. J and her practice; and,
(h) expressing that he believes in retaliation and would retaliate.
15. On consultation with the College, Dr. K agreed to report the interaction to the College pursuant to section 32.2 of the Health Professions Act and in accordance with Dr. K's reporting obligations as the Plaintiff's supervisor.
16. By letter dated July 10, 2018, NHA informed the Plaintiff that it would no longer continue to sponsor him as a Provisionally Licensed Psychiatrist as of September 30, 2018. This decision was based on the Plaintiff's conduct with his supervisors. There were no more qualified local physicians who were willing to supervise the Plaintiff.
[9] In turn, in 2018 the plaintiff filed formal complaints with respect to his supervisors and colleagues.
2. These proceedings
[10] The proposed amended pleadings frame their claims in breach of contract, breach of duty of good faith, negligence, constructive dismissal, breach of "duties of an employer", breach of the "implied duty of decency, civility, respect, and dignity", and defamation. The allegations are based on facts that occurred from roughly February 2017 to February 2020.
[11] The plaintiff filed his original notice of civil claim on July 13, 2020. On September 22, 2020, he filed his amended statement of claim, as of right.
[12] Although over five years have passed since the commencement of this claim, there has been little progress. Lists of documents have been exchanged. One witness has been discovered. No trial date has been set.
[13] In pleadings motions such as the present, the courts will sometimes exhibit some leniency towards self-represented plaintiffs unfamiliar with the basic rules of pleadings: Testar Estate v. Hean Wylie & Company (Hean Wylie Peach DeStefanis), 2024 BCCA 129 at para 36. In the present case, Dr Afridi has been represented throughout by counsel: Alliance Lawyers Oberoi Haben Malli LLP.
C. LAW
1. Amendments
[14] Generally, amendments will be granted liberally to ensure that the real issues between the parties are identified and tried: Kapila v. Merit Interior Designs (Duncan), 2023 BCSC 1076 at para 37.
[15] As set out by Justice G.P. Weatherill in Peterson v. 446690 B.C. Ltd., 2014 BCSC 1531 at para 37:
[37] Finally, the general principles arising on an application to amend pleading can be summarized as follows:
(a) Amendment to pleadings ought to be allowed unless pleadings fail to disclose a cause of action or defence: McNaughton v. Baker, [1988] 24 B.C.L.R. (2nd) 17.
(b) Amendments are usually permitted to determine the issues between the parties and ought to be allowed unless it would cause prejudice to party's ability to defend an action: Levi v. Petaquilla Minerals Ltd., 2012 BCSC 776.
(c) The party resisting an amendment must prove prejudice to preclude an amendment, and mere, potential prejudice is insufficient to preclude an amendment: Jones v. Lululemon Athletica Inc. 2008 BCSC 719.
(d) Costs are the general means of protecting against prejudice unless it would be a wholly inadequate remedy.
(e) Courts should only disallow an amendment as a last resort: Jones, McNaughton, Innoventure S & K Holdings Ltd. et al. v. Innoventure (Tri-Cities) Holdings Ltd. et al., 2006 BCSC 1567.
[16] In Jones v. Bank of Nova Scotia, 2018 BCCA 381 at para 35, the Court of Appeal sets out factors that a chambers judge should consider when determining whether to permit amendments of pleadings that are allegedly bound to fail:
a) the degree to which the pleadings are deficient;
b) the extent to which the deficiencies may be addressed by an obvious or straightforward amendment to the pleadings;
c) the apparent merit of the claim; and
d) prejudice by dismissal of the claim.
[17] A court will refuse to grant an amendment if the proposed pleading does not disclose a reasonable cause of action: Argo Ventures Inc. v. Choi, 2019 BCSC 86 at para 7. Nor will a court permit useless amendments: One West Holdings Ltd. v. The Owners, Strata Plan LMS2995, 2019 BCSC 707 at para 76.
2. Striking pleadings
[18] Rule 9‑5(1) sets out grounds to strike a pleading:
Scandalous, frivolous or vexatious matters
(1) At any stage of a proceeding, the court may order to be struck out or amended the whole or any part of a pleading, petition or other document on the ground that
(a) it discloses no reasonable claim or defence, as the case may be,
(b) it is unnecessary, scandalous, frivolous or vexatious,
(c) it may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial or hearing of the proceeding, or
(d) it is otherwise an abuse of the process of the court,
and the court may pronounce judgment or order the proceeding to be stayed or dismissed and may order the costs of the application to be paid as special costs.
[19] The test for allowing amendments is the same as that for striking pleadings pursuant to Rule 9-5(1)(a): Health Sciences Association of British Columbia v. Hewitt Associates Corp., 2019 BCSC 208 at paras 67-68.
[20] Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959 at 980 states with respect to Rule 19(24)(a), the predecessor of Rule 9-5(1)(a):
Thus, the test in Canada governing the application of provisions like Rule 19(24)(a) of the British Columbia Rules of Court is the same as the one that governs an application under R.S.C. O. 18, r. 19: assuming that the facts as stated in the statement of claim can be proved, is it "plain and obvious" that the plaintiff's statement of claim discloses no reasonable cause of action? As in England, if there is a chance that the plaintiff might succeed, then the plaintiff should not be "driven from the judgment seat". Neither the length and complexity of the issues, the novelty of the cause of action, nor the potential for the defendant to present a strong defence should prevent the plaintiff from proceeding with his or her case. Only if the action is certain to fail because it contains a radical defect ranking with the others listed in Rule 19(24) of the British Columbia Rules of Court should the relevant portions of a plaintiff's statement of claim be struck out under Rule 19(24)(a).
[emphasis added]
[21] Citizens for Foreign Aid Reform Inc. v. Canadian Jewish Congress, (1999) 36 CPC (4th) 266, 1999 CanLII 5860 (BCSC) ["CFAR"], sets out principles governing subsections (b) and (c):
[45] To succeed on an application under Rule 19(24) (b) or (c) it must be established that it is "plain and obvious" that the pleading offends either or both provisions.
[46] The authorities provide little guidance as to what constitutes pleadings that are "unnecessary", "scandalous", "frivolous" or "embarrassing". However some principles do emerge.
[47] Irrelevancy and embarrassment are both established when pleadings are so confusing that it is difficult to understand what is being pleaded . . . An "embarrassing" and "scandalous" pleading is one that is so irrelevant that it will involve the parties in useless expense and will prejudice the trial of the action by involving them in a dispute apart from the issues . . . An allegation which is scandalous will not be struck if it is relevant to the proceedings. It will only be struck if irrelevant as well as scandalous . . . A pleading is "unnecessary" or "vexatious" if it does not go to establishing the plaintiff's cause of action or does not advance any claim known in law . . . A pleading that is superfluous will not be struck out if it is not necessarily unnecessary or otherwise objectionable . . . A pleading is "frivolous" if it is obviously unsustainable, not in the sense that it lacks an evidentiary basis, but because of the doctrine of estoppel . . .
[authorities omitted]
[22] CFAR also sets out principles governing subsection (d):
[52] The ambit of abuse of process is very broad. Abuse of process may be found where proceedings involve a deception of the court or constitute a mere sham; where process of the court is not being fairly or honestly used, or is employed for some ulterior or improper purpose; proceedings which are without foundation or serve no useful purpose: Babavic v. Babowech (3 September 1993) Vancouver Registry C931968 (B.C.S.C.). In the case at bar, I am unable to find that the plaintiff's pleadings in paragraphs 9 and 10 of the Statement of Claim are vexatious and without merit, brought with the sole motive and intent to harass the defendants and to interfere with their ability to defend the action.
[23] In R. v. Imperial Tobacco Canada Ltd., 2011 SCC 42, the Supreme Court of Canada explained the important housekeeping function provided by civil procedure strike provisions:
[20] This promotes two goods -- efficiency in the conduct of the litigation and correct results. Striking out claims that have no reasonable prospect of success promotes litigation efficiency, reducing time and cost. The litigants can focus on serious claims, without devoting days and sometimes weeks of evidence and argument to claims that are in any event hopeless. The same applies to judges and juries, whose attention is focused where it should be -- on claims that have a reasonable chance of success. The efficiency gained by weeding out unmeritorious claims in turn contributes to better justice. The more the evidence and arguments are trained on the real issues, the more likely it is that the trial process will successfully come to grips with the parties' respective positions on those issues and the merits of the case.
[emphasis added]
3. Pleadings
[24] Rule 3-1(2) sets out the requirements of a notice of civil claim:
Contents of notice of civil claim
(2) A notice of civil claim must do the following:
(a) set out a concise statement of the material facts giving rise to the claim;
(b) set out the relief sought by the plaintiff against each named defendant;
(c) set out a concise summary of the legal basis for the relief sought;
(d) set out the proposed place of trial;
(e) if the plaintiff sues or a defendant is sued in a representative capacity, show in what capacity the plaintiff sues or the defendant is sued;
(f) provide the data collection information required in the appendix to the form;
(g) otherwise comply with Rule 3-7.
[25] Courts have emphasised the need to be clear and concise in drafting a notice of civil claim, in reference to Rule 3-1(2). In Sahyoun v. Ho, 2013 BCSC 1143 at para 23, Justice Voith, before his translation to the Court of Appeal, stated that "brevity and lucidity are important" in meeting the basic requirements of a notice of civil claim.
[26] The leading case on pleadings requirements was also authored by Voith JA, by then on the Court of Appeal: Mercantile Office Systems Private Ltd. v. Worldwide Warranty Life Services Inc., 2021 BCCA 362. There, Voith JA reminds us that pleadings are not a free-for-all forum for larding on evidence and argument:
[21] Pleadings are foundational. They guide the litigation process . . .
. . .
[23] For the court, pleadings serve the ultimate function of defining the issues of fact and law that will be determined by the court. In order for the court to fairly decide the issues before them, the pleadings must state the material facts succinctly. . . . They must be organized in such a way that the court can understand what issues the court will be called upon to decide. . . .
. . .
[44] Nevertheless, none of a notice of claim, a response to civil claim, and a counterclaim is a story. Each pleading contemplates and requires a reasonably disciplined exercise that is governed, in many instances in mandatory terms, by the Rules and the relevant authorities. Each requires the drafting party to "concisely" set out the "material facts" that give rise to the claim or that relate to the matters raised by the claim. None of these pleadings are permitted to contain evidence or argument.
[45] What constitutes a material fact is well understood. Material facts are the elements essential to formulate a claim or a defence. In Danyluk v. Ainsworth Technologies Inc, 2001 SCC 44, Justice Binnie said:
[54] A cause of action has traditionally been defined as comprising every fact which it would be necessary for the plaintiff to prove, if disputed, in order to support his or her right to the judgment of the court . Establishing each such fact (sometimes referred to as material facts) constitutes a precondition to success.
. . .
[49] The judge's assertion that a pleading can contain the evidence, as opposed to the material facts, that a party may be entitled to establish at trial is an error in principle. . . .
. . .
[52] Such evidence and arguments are unrelated to the material facts Warranty Life was required to plead and have no place in the Response.
. . .
[58] I am of the view that the Response and Counterclaim suffer from the numerous and pervasive difficulties that I have described. These difficulties cause the Response and Counterclaim to be prolix and both confusing and inconsistent in various respects. They offend various mandatory requirements of the Rules and they frustrate the important objects that are served by proper pleadings.
[emphasis added; some authorities omitted]
[27] Kindylides v. Does, 2020 BCCA 330, upheld this court's striking of a claim that failed to plead the material facts necessary to support its causes of action. The Court provides a useful definition of necessary material facts:
[32] Much of what is contained in the NOCC does not constitute "material facts" as defined in Young v. Borzoni et al, 2007 BCCA 16 [Young], citing Delaney & Friends Cartoon Productions Ltd. v. Radical Entertainment Inc. et al, 2005 BCSC 371:
[20] "[facts] that [are] essential in order to formulate a complete cause of action. If a material fact is omitted, a cause of action is not effectively pleaded."
[33] Rather the NOCC contains statements which, as pled, are incapable of proof and/or are based on speculation or assumptions. Such statements are not material facts: Samuda v. Knight, 2019 BCSC 133 at paras. 22-24; Imperial Tobacco at para. 22; Young at paras. 30-32.
[emphasis added]
[28] Bald assertions or conclusions of law are not material facts: Kindylides at para 34, quoting Owimar v. Stewart, 2019 BCSC 1198. The pleadings must be looked at as a whole to ensure that the pleadings define the issues with sufficient precision to make the pretrial and trial proceedings both manageable and fair:
[34] I agree with the observations of MacDonald J. (in Chambers) in Owimar v. Stewart, 2019 BCSC 1198 when she stated:
[19] Care must be taken to avoid undermining the role of pleadings in identifying the issues by allowing a party to plead bald assertions or mere conclusory statements of law. A bald assertion of a conclusion is not a pleading of material fact:
[18] There is no bright line between material facts and bald allegations, nor between pleadings of material facts and the prohibition on pleading of evidence. They are points on a continuum, and it is the responsibility of a motions judge, looking at the pleadings as a whole, to ensure that the pleadings define the issues with sufficient precision to make the pre-trial and trial proceedings both manageable and fair.
[19] What constitutes a material fact is determined in light of the cause of action and the damages sought to be recovered. The plaintiff must plead, in summary form but with sufficient detail, the constituent elements of each cause of action or legal ground raised. The pleading must tell the defendant who, when, where, how and what gave rise to its liability.
Mancuso v. Canada (Minister of National Health and Welfare), 2015 FCA 227 at paras. 18-19 & 27.
[emphasis added]
[29] Similarly, a claim may be struck if it does not "set out a concise statement of the material facts giving rise to the claim", as required by Rule 3-1(2)(a).
D. DISCUSSION AND DECISION
[30] The plaintiff argues that the Caldwell J order allowing the plaintiff to attempt to present a properly pleaded amended claim is a complete answer to the defendants' strike application. The plaintiff further argues that his purported compliance with that order is also a complete answer to the defendants' strike application, based on abuse of process and other grounds that were before Caldwell J. The defendants advanced largely the same strike arguments before Caldwell J. The fact that Caldwell J declined to strike the claim, and gave the plaintiff one last chance to amend implicitly indicated that those arguments were not persuasive then and should not be renewed now. It also implicitly indicated that if there was meticulous compliance with his direction, then the amendments could proceed and the claim could continue.
[31] I reject this argument. Caldwell J appropriately followed the usual course of giving the offending litigant a chance to provide a proper pleading: the usual remedy for a pleading impugned on a strike application or a rejected application to amend. That decision obviated the need to dive deep into the other bases for the strike application. At para 14, Caldwell J expressly authorised the defendants to renew their strike application to be heard at the same time as the amendment application. This dovetails wholly with the "last chance" admonition: the plaintiff's pleadings and case were on death row. Further, this present consideration of the resultant last chance pleading adds yet another prolonging chapter in this proceeding, and provides further grounds in itself to strike the claim.
[32] The plaintiff also argues that Caldwell J's "last chance" direction is obiter dicta, and not binding on this court, citing Bilin v. Sidhu, 2017 BCCA 429. I agree that I am not bound by that direction, just as no judge is wholly bound by a colleague's previous direction that an adjourned matter be heard peremptorily. I will consider these issues and applications unfettered by that prior direction of Caldwell J. That said, it would take an extraordinary change of circumstances for me to depart from the effects of a clear judicial direction, particularly one based on largely the exact same materials and arguments before me today.
[33] The plaintiff points to the jurisprudence setting out the traditionally liberal approach to amendments, as well as the general approach of providing a party an opportunity to amend, rather than striking a pleading for good, without a chance to provide a properly pleaded amended claim or defence. He argues that these principles especially apply to the admittedly novel claims he advances, such as that based on what he pleads to be a relationship with the Northern Health Authority akin to employment. The plaintiff cites Sommer v. Coast Capital Savings Credit Union, 2013 BCSC 881, Cicada Ventures Ltd. v. KGHM Ajax Mining Inc., 2023 BCSC 1746, and McMillan v. McMillan, 2014 BCSC 546.
[34] In the present circumstances, of course, the plaintiff has already been provided an opportunity to amend his claim in a properly pleaded form, in the face of a strike application. Justice Caldwell did not only give the plaintiff an opportunity to amend: again he did so with the unambiguous language that it was the plaintiff's "'last chance' to amend his claim to an acceptable form which properly identifies his claims and abides the Rules and law relating to pleadings": para 14. Justice Caldwell provided that final warning after reciting the various deficiencies of the plaintiff's existing and proposed amended claim.
[35] If that judicial admonishment and warning was not sufficient to prompt the plaintiff to provide impeccable pleadings and particulars today, the applications and arguments of the defendants, before Caldwell J and before me, setting out in detail the manifold manifest deficiencies in the plaintiff's pleadings, themselves provide a comprehensive list of what the pleadings now before the court should and should not contain. Those arguments included case law setting out that certain advanced claims, such as those based in discrimination, bad faith, and harassment, are not torts. In striking the pleading in the Canadian Society for the Advancement of Science in Public Policy v. British Columbia, 2025 BCSC 2051 ["CSASPP"], the Court noted that the plaintiff persisted with defective pleadings even after being expressly forewarned of those deficiencies: see para 146.
[36] I agree that in these remarkable circumstances, and in light of the express judicial "last chance", the proposed pleadings presented by the plaintiffs today ought to have been meticulous, in complete and unimpeachable adherence to the Rules of Court and the rules of pleadings.
[37] The plaintiff argues that he has, in fact, "meticulously" complied with the Justice Caldwell order. He argues that he has adhered to the directive by:
A. Pleading Material Facts: Clearly outlining the specific tortious acts, including the suppression of exculpatory evidence and the use of discriminatory bias by the Defendants' agents (Proposed Amended Claim, paras. 45, 57-60).
B. Refining Legal Claims: Correctly framing the negligence claims, the statutory immunity exception for bad faith, and the intentional torts (Misfeasance in Public Office).
[38] Unfortunately, the proposed second amended notice of civil claim falls miles short of this express requisite expectation of meticulous compliance with the Rules and the rules of pleadings.
[39] Following the lead of Caldwell J, these reasons will not attempt to identify all of the deficiencies in the proposed pleading.
[40] Roughly 85 percent of the proposed pleading is underlined, to indicate that it is brand new and not carried over from the previous pleading. At the same time, the proposed pleading contains no strike-through text or carets ("^") to indicate paragraphs and words that have been removed, in breach of Rule 6-1(3). It is accordingly impossible to discern what portions are truly new, in content or in concept.
[41] As we will see later in the proceedings, not only is this lack of clarity profoundly prejudicial to the defendants, and for that matter the Court, it is ultimately prejudicial to the plaintiff himself.
[42] Far from the Rule 3-1(2), Mercantile, and jurisprudential requirements of concision and brevity, the proposed pleadings are prolix and bloated. This bloat is partly attributable to the repetition of particulars and facts throughout the proposed pleadings, again out of sequence. What would appear to be a claim primarily based in negligence, breach of contract, and defamation, spans 151 paragraphs over 29 pages. It is larded with adjectives, adverbs, and superfluous prose. It is argumentative, and, indeed, resembles less a pleading than a trial argument, complete with extensive citation of case law. It is unnecessary and inappropriate to cite case law in a pleading, except where that case is shorthand for a legal principle (e.g. Rylands v Fletcher): Pyper v. The Law Society of British Columbia, 2017 BCCA 410 at para 49; British Columbia Teachers' Federation v. British Columbia, 2012 BCSC 1722, paras 14-15.
[43] The narrative of events and material facts is not pleaded in sequence. The timeline jumps around, making it extremely difficult to follow. As an illustration of this pervasive fogginess, it is not even clear from the proposed pleading whether and when the plaintiff stopped working with the Health Authority.
[44] The pleadings sets out various communications between the plaintiff and the Health Authority and the College. We suddenly find ourselves on Vancouver Island and then we find ourselves back in communications with the defendants. A reader of the pleadings would have grave difficulty in discerning the basic facts alleged, and the basic dates alleged, let alone how they relate to the purported claims advanced.
[45] I will note a few other problems and ambiguities with respect to specific claims purportedly advanced in the proposed pleadings under various bolded headings.
[46] With respect to negligence, the plaintiff advances various complaints about the defendants' conduct. The claim does not plead clearly the source of the various duties that the plaintiff claims the defendants have breached: whether they are grounded in statute, common law, contract, or otherwise. Nor does it set out the content of those duties or the content of the standard of care that the defendants apparently failed to meet.
[47] Of course, it may well be that some or all of these possible foundations for the negligence claim are intended in the pleading. The pleading does not make that clear, as required, as a fundamental precept.
[48] To add to the confusion, the entirety of paragraph 69, under the heading of "Negligence", reads more as a plea of an intentional tort, rather than of negligence: "[d]eliberately concealing a complaint"; "[f]ostering a toxic environment"; "[m]aliciously obstructing employment"; "[u]nlawfully threatening co-workers"; and "[p]roviding false information". These assertions would not appear to be claims in negligence, notwithstanding the heading under which they are found. Apart from defamation, no intentional tort is expressly pleaded anywhere in the proposed claim.
[49] Finally, with respect to the negligence section, the plaintiff does not plead a factual basis for a finding that the Health Authority or the College may be vicariously liable for any acts or omissions caused by its members. This is not a notional failure. The pleading is provided in the face of specific well‑established jurisprudence that physicians are typically considered independent contractors of a health authority, rather than employees: Bahinipaty v. Vancouver Coastal Health Authority (Vancouver General Hospital), 2019 BCSC 2112 at paras 17-19, Cupo v. Brady, 2013 BCSC 1749 at para 6, and Shannahan v. Fraser Health Authority, 2010 BCSC 144 at paras 9-13. Where physicians are not direct employees of a hospital -- that is, employees of a health authority -- the hospital and health authority is not vicariously liable for their conduct. Accordingly, the College cannot be vicariously liable for those same physicians, even if material facts of liability had hypothetically been provided.
[50] With respect to the pleadings of breach of contract, found in Part 1, paras 12-17, 46-48, and Part 3, paras 2-8, it is unclear whether the plaintiff advances a claim for breach of contract in relation to a private contract between the Health Authority and the plaintiff, or an employment contract which engages a different legal framework. The pleadings refer to several different contracts, as well as contracts "the terms of which are found in the Health Professions Act (the "HPA"), the Defendant College's Bylaws, and the common law (collectively, the 'CPSBC Contract')": Part 1, para 16. Further, at Part 1, para 12, the plaintiff asserts that the contract between himself and the Health Authority "was one of direct employment or, in the alternative, was analogous to employment." He provides no material facts to support this vague and odd pleading, which is more along the lines of an argument.
[51] The plaintiff does not provide particulars of the material terms of each contract, as required, or tie those terms, or the contracts themselves, to the breaches he alleges, or the remedies he seeks. While he lists various "express and implied terms", he does not indicate the source or basis of any of those terms. In a simple contract dispute with a single written contract that provides a solid, clear, and unambiguous point of reference in the pleadings, the court may turn a blind eye to such laxity. With multiple amorphous and imprecise references to contracts involving two different defendants, it is wholly inexcusable and confusing.
[52] I agree that the proposed pleading is prejudicially unclear to the defendants, who would be hard pressed to identify which contract is alleged to have been breached and how.
[53] Part 1, paras 18-45 asserts a "coordinated campaign of bad faith and discrimination", while paras 78-92 asserts "bad faith, malice, and discrimination." Part 3, paras 9-14 asserts that the campaign of bad faith and discrimination breached the defendants' duty of good faith, citing Bhasin v. Hrynew, 2014 SCC 71.
[54] It is well established that bad faith is not an independent cause of action. Breach of a duty of good faith does not stand alone without a breach of some other underlying duty owed to the plaintiff, be it in tort, statute, or contract: Castrillo v. Workplace Safety and Insurance Board, 2017 ONCA 121 at para 74, and Alberta v. Elder Advocates of Alberta Society, 2011 SCC 24 at para 78.
[55] The pleadings of material facts in Part 1 regarding bad faith are rambling, lengthy, repetitive, and out of sequence. They are peppered with impermissible argument and evidence. In an odd and prolix manner, the particulars are repeated in two locations under the Part 1 factual basis of the pleading.
[56] Those material facts are also confusing as they purport to be particulars of intentional acts of bad faith, but on several occasions refer to "negligence", or the "bad faith" actions of individuals and entities who are strangers to this action. I agree that the pleadings leave the defendants guessing at which particulars apply to the generalised pleadings of bad faith, especially given that those Part 1 assertions remain unanchored to any particular legal right.
[57] In Part 3, the plaintiff pleads that there is a duty of good faith that arises from contractual performance. But he fails to identify in which of many potential contractual relationships such a duty arises: a deficiency exacerbated here by the multiple asserted contracts, some of which are of a particularly amorphous implied nature, as set out above. The proposed pleading is unclear as to the source of any contractual relationship, let alone the specific terms of that contract.
[58] I agree that the bad faith pleadings simply read as a litany of complaints the plaintiff seeks to advance, unconnected to any legal basis for relief.
[59] That section also pleads discrimination.
[60] As with bad faith, it is well established that there is no common law tort of discrimination, a matter to be addressed by the British Columbia Human Rights Code, RSBC 1996, c. 210, ss 7, 21: Seneca College v. Bhadauria, [1981] 2 SCR 181 at 195; Honda Canada Inc. v. Keays, 2008 SCC 39 at paras 63-65.
[61] Accordingly, allegations of discrimination cannot be made as particulars of a breach of standard of care in a negligence claim. Further, discrimination cannot support a claim for punitive damages: Honda at paras 63-65; Campbell v. Vancouver Police Board (No. 4), 2019 BCHRT 275 at para 150.
[62] As such, pleadings alleging discrimination are regularly struck as disclosing no reasonable cause of action, being unnecessary, scandalous, frivolous, and vexatious: Ajvazi v. Century 21 Prudential Estates, 2024 BCSC 276 at paras 62-65, 68; Hall v. Trueblue, DBA Labour Ready, 2017 BCSC 2004 at paras 34-36; Bulwer v. Canadian Mental Health Association and others, 2016 BCSC 1110 at paras 12-15.
[63] This pleading is especially egregious as the plaintiff has already brought a claim for discrimination before the Human Rights Tribunal, which was dismissed by virtue of his own reprehensible conduct directed against the other parties and against the Tribunal members themselves, as will be discussed further below.
[64] Part 1, paras 93-97 pleads "harassment".
[65] Again, similarly, there is no recognised tort of harassment: Anderson v. Double M Construction, 2021 BCSC 1473 at paras 59-62; Simpson v. Rebel News Network Ltd., 2022 BCSC 1160 at para 55. Understandably, in the Part 3 legal basis, the plaintiff does not even attempt to plead a legal basis for this claim.
[66] Part 1, paras 104-109, and Part 3, paras 32-36 introduces a wholly new claim in defamation.
[67] Defamation proceedings are technical in nature and pleading‑dependent. A plaintiff must be precise in respect of the alleged defamatory words and their publication and meaning. The law requires that a defamation pleading specify "the publication made by each defendant, the words published by that defendant, . . . the time and place of the publication, the manner of the publication, and to whom the publication was made." A plaintiff cannot rely on a vague, general statement of the defamatory words: Christian Advocacy Society of Greater Vancouver v. Arthur, 2013 BCSC 1542 at paras 108-110; Cooper v. Hennan, 2005 ABQB 709 at para 18; Nu Fibre Inc. v. Ishkanian, 2013 BCSC 1255 at para 20; Fraser v. Central Ready-Mix Ltd., [1999] BCJ No. 2061 at para 12, CanLII 2066 (SC).
[68] The proposed defamation claim fails to particularise the impugned statements, the audience for those statements, the dates of those statements, the meaning of those statements, or whether those meanings are express or implied. Part 1 makes various references to statements that could in theory be what the defamation claim is based upon. Part 3 seems to make passing reference to some of those statements, but hedges its bets by making reference to "including without limitation ", inferring that he may in fact be suing on statements that are not pleaded at all.
[69] The deficiencies in the defamation pleading are particularly egregious, given that the defendants' arguments and communications before and at the Caldwell J hearing specifically set out these necessary principles of defamation pleading. The plaintiff was specifically put on notice of the deficiencies in its then‑proposed defamation claim: para 8. Instead of using those cautions as a checklist to ensure meticulous compliance in his last chance pleading, he completely ignored those admonitions.
[70] The proposed defamation pleadings, like the other claims, raise acute limitation period concerns. Insofar as the pleadings contain passing references to various utterances, these occurred between 2017 and 2019: the limitation period for these new claims has long since expired. In several letters he authored in 2019, the plaintiff himself refers to those alleged statements, indicating that he discovered them in early 2019, and could well have brought this claim in a timely manner.
[71] In this, I agree that the prejudice to the defendants is also particularly acute. These allegations of a considerably different and new claim will require re‑investigation, as well as development of any applicable defences, including the evidence‑heavy defence of truth or justification. The proposed amendment comes after several rounds of document disclosure and the examination for discovery of a representative of the College, which may well have to be repeated based upon this new cause of action.
[72] We will return to limitation issues later in these reasons.
[73] Part 1, paras 98-103, and Part 3, paras 22-31 introduce a new claim in "employment"/ "constructive dismissal".
[74] As set out above, it is not clear whether the proposed employment law claim is based upon the pleaded Health Authority contract, or a statutory duty, or an administrative law right, or a relationship "analogous to employment." It is accordingly also unclear whether the alleged breaches of the Health Authority contract, or "bad faith" existing elsewhere in the pleading, relate to the proposed employment law claim.
[75] As with defamation, this new claim suffers immediate and profound limitation problems. The moments of the alleged wrongful dismissal or when the plaintiff ended his relationship with the Health Authority, neither of which could possibly be a secret to the plaintiff, and both of which appear to have occurred around September 2018, have long since passed. Any claim in relationship to employment law would, could, and should have been discoverable upon the date on which the plaintiff believed himself to have been dismissed. The plaintiff provides no explanation for the delay, and it is hard to conceive how he could provide any reasonable explanation for this delayed claim, in light of bringing his present lawsuit in 2020.
[76] As with defamation, wrongful dismissal is an entirely different cause of action from those previously pleaded. A defence to a wrongful dismissal claim requires further investigation and development of the issue of whether the plaintiff was an employee or independent contractor, whether dismissal is even an applicable term for the situation at bar, whether there may have been just cause for dismissal. It also introduces an entirely new theory and measure of damages. I agree that the prejudice to the Health Authority in this respect is acute in responding to an evidence‑heavy claim that may well profoundly rely upon the recollection and evidence of its employees and associates, some of which may well have departed or retired in the seven ensuing years.
[77] In conclusion, I agree that the proposed pleading should be struck under each of the strike subsections advanced by the defendants.
[78] Specifically, under subrule (a), the proposed pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action: the proposed pleadings are replete with claims that disclose no reasonable cause of action known at law, as well as other proposed new pleadings that would, on their face, appear to be destined to fail.
[79] With respect to subrules (b) and (d), the proposed pleadings fail to meet the basic requirements of pleadings: the proposed pleadings are vexatious, prolix, and constitute an abuse of process of this court.
[80] Before me is the plaintiff's fourth attempt to plead his claims in proper form. The plaintiff argues that it should only be considered the third attempt and that only filed versions of the pleadings--that is, the initial pleading and the amended pleading--should be considered in tallying up the number of times the plaintiff has had an opportunity to provide a proper set of pleadings to the court. I disagree. Considerable court time was consumed, both during the hearing and no doubt, in considering the arguments after the hearing, in considering the inadequate proposed pleading presented in the hearing before Justice Caldwell. No doubt tens of hours of the defendants' time were consumed in sifting through those prolix and confusing pleadings.
[81] The plaintiff has put four versions of its pleadings which he purports to be sufficient on which to ground its claim. The present proposed pleading is the fourth attempt to put forward a claim that complies with basic jurisprudential and rules pleadings requirements, and a fourth attempt that has profoundly failed to comply with those rules.
[82] The proposed pleadings remain prolix, vexatious, and confusing. In this, the recent words of CSASPP apply almost equally in the present case:
[422] The plaintiff has had eight opportunities and several years to decide what claim it wishes to advance.
[423] The words of WCB v. Sort, where certification was denied, with no leave to amend, speak equally to the present application:
[185] In my opinion, there is a significant difference between an amended pleading clarifying or modifying claims which have been properly certified and allowing amendments to cure one which is fundamentally deficient. That is particularly so if the proposed amendments include entirely new causes of action. While the courts "tend to be generous in making available the possibility of amendments to fine tune the pleadings and to bring clarification to obscure issues", it is also the case that "the essentials of a cause of action must be pleaded else the pleadings may be found to be fatally lacking"; also, in considering this issue, the court will consider "the length of time the plaintiff has had to 'get it right'": Sandhu [v. HSBC Finance Mortgages Inc., 2016 BCCA 301] at para. 44.
[emphasis added; authorities omitted]
[83] In some ways, however, the fact that there were eight iterations of the claim in CSASSP, versus the four in the present case, was more forgivable in CSASSP. There the Court acknowledged that some degree of amendment was understandable, given the shifting nature of the health restrictions imposed during the COVID pandemic, as well as the steady stream of jurisprudence in relation to those restrictions and similar restrictions across the country: see para 47. Here, the facts on which the claim is based arose almost entirely before the commencement of his claim: the plaintiff was fully in a position to plead his claim in a proper manner when he started this proceeding, let alone five years after the commencement of this proceeding.
[84] Notwithstanding the liberal approach to amendments, in their own right and as a remedy for a struck pleading, I agree that the only just result is to strike the claim without leave to amend, and to deny the plaintiff yet another chance to provide a proper pleading. To provide the plaintiff with another chance would fail to give full faith and credit to the express and unambiguous pronouncement of my brother Caldwell J. The plaintiff's failure to meticulously comply with Justice Caldwell's last‑chance order is sufficient in itself to deny the plaintiff's application and grant those of the defendants.
[85] With an eye to the factors set out in Jones and Testar:
a) the pleadings remain profoundly deficient;
b) the deficiencies cannot be addressed by an obvious or straightforward amendment to the pleadings;
c) the claim has little merit, in light of the limitations issues and many other legal impediments identified above; and
d) expiry of the limitation period for many of the claims has inflicted prima facie prejudice on the defendants, for which actual prejudice is also in evidence.
[86] To further paraphrase Testar, in light of the history of this matter, the past opportunities to correct the pleadings, alongside the powerful fillip of the Caldwell J warning, I have no confidence that a further opportunity to amend will correct the manifold defects in this claim. To quote a definition often incorrectly attributed to Albert Einstein, "insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results."[2]
[87] While I need not categorically or comprehensively determine the following issues, they further indicate why this claim should be struck as bound to fail.
[88] First, as noted above, the claims are based on facts that occurred between five and seven years ago, and as such are presumptively statute-barred by the two‑year limitation period.
[89] It is granted that the facts underlying the present proposed pleadings may have been pleaded in some form in the initial July 2020 notice of civil claim, such that some of the claims may not, in fact, be statute-barred. Where the proposed amendments flow from facts previously pleaded, even if in support of a new or alternative claim or remedy, there will generally be no issue as to the potential expiry of a limitation period: Taylor v. Blenz The Coffee Company Ltd., 2019 BCSC 906 at paras 35 and 44, citing Swiss Reinsurance Company v. Camarin Limited, 2018 BCCA 122 at para 21. A proposed amendment will only be refused after the expiry of a limitation period where it seeks to advance a fundamentally different claim: 1100997 Ontario Limited v. North Elgin Centre Inc., 2016 ONCA 848 at para 22. See generally the discussion in British Columbia Securities Commission v. Pasquill, 2023 BCSC 2150 at paras 89-93, aff'd 2025 BCCA 287.
[90] The plaintiff, however, has made no attempt to establish that those facts were already pleaded to any extent in the initial claim. As the vast majority of paragraphs in the proposed pleading are underlined, indicating new additions, presumptively the facts alleged are new, arriving only in 2025, well after the expiration of the limitation periods. It is not the role of the court on an application such as the present to sift through and compare two already dismayingly prolix and confusing versions of the pleading to determine if the factual essence of each claim existed in some form in the earlier iteration of the pleading. In this hearing, the Court expressly suggested that plaintiff's counsel provide a table linking proposed 2025 pleadings to existing 2020 pleadings for this very purpose, but no such table was ever provided.
[91] Again, it was the plaintiff who underlined the vast majority of the proposed pleading, while at the same time leaving roughly 15 percent of it un‑underlined, to indicate a carry-over from past pleadings. The plaintiff's own drafting itself indicates, according to the Rules and conventions with respect to amendments, that the vast majority of its claims are brand new and thus presumptively statute‑barred.
[92] Similarly, it might be open to the plaintiff to argue that certain claims are not statute-barred, as he only discovered the claims, or the facts on which the claims are based, later, thus delaying the ticking of the limitation clock and the tolling of the limitation period. But the plaintiff has not presented the Court with any basis to consider discoverability. The plaintiff has presented no affidavit to that effect. He has not even provided a filed reply that affirmatively asserts discoverability in reply to the defendants' expressly pleaded limitation defences in their responses. Those responses were filed in 2020: the plaintiff has had five years to consider the limitation defences, and provide a reply, or evidence or discoverability.
[93] Kapila at para 40 and Chouinard v. O'Connor, 2011 BCCA 161 at para 20, citing Letvad v. Fenwick, 2000 BCCA 630 at para 29, set out that where a plaintiff adds new causes of action after the prima facie expiry of a limitation period, the court's discretion whether to allow the amendments is guided by the following:
1. The extent of the delay in bringing the amendments;
2. The reasons for the delay;
3. Any explanation put forward to account for the delay;
4. The degree of prejudice caused by the delay; and
5. The extent of the connection, if any, between existing claims and any proposed new cause of action.
[94] Again, the plaintiff has failed to address these jurisprudentially-mandated factors, by affidavit or otherwise, except by way of an affidavit presented on the second day making passing reference to various freedom of information requests to the Ministry of Health.
[95] The passages underlined by the plaintiff in his proposed amended pleadings are presumptively statute-barred. It is appropriate to bar these professed new pleadings, advanced five years after the fact, on that basis.
[96] Apart from indicating that many, if not most, of the newly‑pleaded claims are bound to fail, the prima facie expiration of limitation periods also speaks to the court's discretion as to whether to allow an amendment; the expiration of a limitation period prima facie inflicts prejudice. For the College, at least, this prejudice is tangible and not merely notional; two key witnesses, including its representative at the discovery, have since retired.
[97] In addition to echoing the Health Authority's submissions, the College raises two specific arguments as to why the plaintiff's proposed amendments would be bound to fail.
[98] First, the College argues that it is well established that it would not owe the plaintiff, as its registrant, a private law duty of care: Nesbitt v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of British Columbia, 2024 BCSC 1661; Madadi v. British Columbia, 2018 BCSC 1891 at para 108. In Nesbitt, Madam Justice Tucker at para 92 struck the claim after finding that it was plain and obvious that negligence was not a viable cause of action against the College defendants, as the College defendants owed no private‑law duty of care to its registrant, the plaintiff.
[99] While I need not conclusively determine the matter today, nothing in the materials before me indicates that any other result would flow in the present case. Like Dr Nesbitt, the plaintiff is a registrant who alleges, in essence, that the College has been negligent in fulfilling its obligations under the Health Professions Act, RSBC 1996, c. 183. The legislative scheme is identical, and the specific interactions alleged are of a similar nature. Given the well‑established jurisprudence, there would be no need for the court to embark on a full Anns/Cooper analysis, as required for novel considerations of whether sufficient proximity exists.
[100] In her thorough submissions, counsel for the College nonetheless does embark on such a full Anns/Cooper analysis, citing well‑established appellate jurisprudence such as Waterway Houseboats Ltd. v. British Columbia, 2020 BCCA 378 and Wu. v. Vancouver (City), 2019 BCCA 23, which have been decided more recently than the contrary authorities provided by the plaintiff. While I need not determine the issue, I find the Anns/Cooper analysis advanced by the College to be equally persuasive as the initial argument with respect to the gateway analysis of whether a duty of care exists in the first place: both indicate that the claims against the College are almost certain to fail.
[101] Second, the College cites the statutory immunity it and its employees and agents enjoy under two relevant statutes.
[102] The Health Profession Act, s. 24 provides:
Immunity for acts or omissions in good faith
24 (1) No action for damages lies or may be brought against a member of a board or a person acting on behalf of or under the direction of a board or college because of anything done or omitted in good faith
(a) in the performance or intended performance of any duty under this Act, or
(b) in the exercise or intended exercise of any power under this Act.
(2) Subsection (1) does not absolve a college from vicarious liability for an act or omission for which it would be vicariously liable if this section were not in force.
[103] The Disciplinary Authority Protection Act, RSBC 1996, c. 98, s. 1 similarly provides:
Protection for exercise of disciplinary powers
1 If,
(a) by an Act a corporation is established having as one of its purposes the regulation of the profession or occupation practised or carried on by the members of the corporation, and
(b) disciplinary powers over the members of the corporation in respect of their conduct in the profession or occupation regulated are conferred by the Act on the corporation, or on any body of its members, or on any official or member of the corporation,
no liability is incurred by the corporation, or by any body of the members, or by any official or member of the corporation, or by any person retained as counsel, solicitor or agent by the corporation for any act or thing done in the exercise of the disciplinary powers conferred or in doing any act or thing in preparation for the exercise of those powers, unless the act or thing is done maliciously and without reasonable and probable cause.
[104] CSASPP considered the analogous immunity provisions in the Public Health Act, SBC 2008, c 28, s. 92, insulating the Public Health Officer Dr Henry from such claims. The Court applied Weisenburger v. College of Naturopathic Physicians of British Columbia, 2024 BCSC 1047 in confirming their efficacy:
[107] In a strike application, the court should consider and give considerable weight to such immunity provisions. If it is plain and obvious that an immunity applies in light of the facts pleaded, the claim should be struck: Ernst at paras. 19 (per Cromwell J), 67 (per Abella J, concurring), 149 (per McLachlin CJ dissenting); Walkom v. Law Society of British Columbia, 2019 BCCA 391 at para. 19.
[108] In Ernst, the Supreme Court of Canada confirms the efficacy of such immunity clauses, striking a claim seeking Charter damages for alleged breaches of the plaintiff's s. 2(b) expression rights by the Alberta Energy Regulator. The Court notes that such clauses should be read expansively, to protect administrative decision-makers from the distraction of litigation flowing from the decisions they make in their legislatively-designated roles:
[57] Immunity is easily frustrated where the mere pleading of an allegation of bad faith or punitive conduct in a statement of claim can call into question a decision-maker's conduct: Gonzalez, at para. 53. Even qualified immunity undermines the decision-maker's ability to act impartially and independently, as the mere threat of litigation, achieved by artful pleadings, will require the decision-maker to engage with claims brought against him or her. As Lord Denning M.R. held, to be truly free in thought, judges should not be "plagued with allegations of malice or ill-will or bias or anything of the kind": Sirros, at p. 136, cited by Morier, at pp. 739-40.
[109] It is conceded that the Ernst Court was considering the Alberta Energy Regulator, serving a quasi-adjudicative function, with an ostensibly absolute immunity clause. But these words also speak for the most part to the present defendants, legislatively entrusted to carry out their important public functions, here as well as below, in the discussion of factors countervailing against the justice and appropriateness of a Charter damages claim. As noted by the Ernst Court, the immunity clause, there, as here, does not bar judicial oversight and intervention by way of judicial review; it only bars damages claims: para. 33.
. . .
[111] Section 92 is drafted in the broadest possible terms. There is no pleaded allegation that the PH Officer is acting in any other capacity than "in the exercise or intended exercise of a power under this or any other enactment" (s. 92(1)(a)), and it is difficult to conceive how one could plead or prove such a proposition.
[emphasis added]
[105] As indicated in Ernst v. Alberta Energy Regulator, 2017 SCC 1, peppering the pleading with bare assertions of bad faith, such as those in the present proposed pleading, is insufficient to escape the immunities afforded by such provisions. Not only must the plaintiff plead material facts as to "who, when, where, how, and what" gave rise to the College's alleged liability, the plaintiff must also plead sufficient material facts that would allow it to establish bad faith, such as to avoid the express and expansive statutory immunities: Kindylides at paras 32-34; Walkom v. Law Society of British Columbia, 2019 BCCA 391 at paras 33-37.
[106] I turn to some of the grounds put forward by the defendants for striking the claim and denying an amendment based on allegations that the proposed pleading is vexatious or an abuse of process.
[107] Vexatious pleadings include those that do not advance any claim known to law, those brought for an improper purpose (including the harassment and oppression of other parties by multifarious and duplicative proceedings brought for purposes other than the pursuit of legitimate rights), claims that do not establish the plaintiff's pleaded causes of actions, claims where it is obvious that the action cannot succeed, and claims where the action would serve no useful purpose and would be a waste of the court's time and public resources: Owimar v. Warnett, 2018 BCSC 2310 at paras 23-24.
[108] Saskatchewan (Environment) v. Métis Nation - Saskatchewan, 2025 SCC 4, a decision this year of the Supreme Court of Canada, describes the doctrine of abuse of process:
[33] The doctrine of abuse of process is concerned with the administration of justice and fairness (Behn, at para. 41). The doctrine engages the inherent power of the court to prevent misuse of its proceedings in a way that would be manifestly unfair to a party or would in some way bring the administration of justice into disrepute (Toronto (City) v. C.U.P.E., Local 79, 2003 SCC 63, [2003] 3 S.C.R. 77, at para. 37; Behn, at para. 39; Abrametz, at para. 33).
[34] In Abrametz, this Court reiterated that abuse of process is a broad concept that applies in various contexts (para. 34, citing Toronto (City), at para. 36, and Behn, at para. 39). The Court noted that the doctrine of abuse of process is "characterized by its flexibility. It is not encumbered by specific requirements, unlike the concepts of res judicata and issue estoppel" (para. 35, citing Behn, at para. 40, and Toronto (City), at paras. 37-38).
[35] One way in which an abuse of process can arise is by relitigation, that is, "where the litigation before the court is found to be in essence an attempt to relitigate a claim which the court has already determined" (Behn, at para. 40, quoting Canam Enterprises Inc. v. Coles (2000), 51 O.R. (3d) 481 (C.A.), at para. 56, rev'd 2002 SCC 63, [2002] 3 S.C.R. 307; see also Abrametz, at para. 34; and D. J. Lange, The Doctrine of Res Judicata in Canada (5th ed. 2021), at pp. 1-5). Relitigation will be an abuse of process if it violates "such principles as judicial economy, consistency, finality and the integrity of the administration of justice" (Toronto (City), at para. 37; Behn, at para. 41). Where warranted, the doctrine of abuse of process can be relied on to strike pleadings so as to prevent relitigation of an issue (see Behn; Canam Enterprises Inc.).
[109] In Métis Nation, the abuse of process argument was directed towards the multiplicity of proceedings filed:
[38] A multiplicity of proceedings which engage the same issues can amount to an abuse of process. In the foundational case of McHenry v. Lewis (1882), 22 Ch. D. 397, Sir George Jessel observed that: ". . . it is prima facie vexatious to bring two actions where one will do" (p. 400). Examples of where a multiplicity of proceedings has amounted to an abuse of process include: where two parallel class actions involving the same parties were brought in two different jurisdictions (Englund v. Pfizer Canada Inc., 2007 SKCA 62, 284 D.L.R. (4th) 94, at paras. 38-40); where plaintiffs initiated multiple actions claiming Aboriginal and treaty rights over the same land and natural resources (Dixon v. Canada (Attorney General), 2015 ABQB 565); and where the plaintiffs provided "no viable explanation" for bringing a second action that duplicated the issue of ownership of a trade name which encapsulated the original defendants (Cashin Mortgages Inc. v. 2511311 Ontario Ltd., 2024 ONCA 103, 170 O.R. (3d) 107, at para. 14).
[39] However, the fact that there are two or more ongoing legal proceedings which involve the same, or similar, parties or legal issues, is in itself not sufficient for an abuse of process. As this Court recognized in Toronto (City), "[t]here may be instances where relitigation will enhance, rather than impeach, the integrity of the judicial system" (para. 52). Similarly, there may be instances where parties have a valid reason for bringing separate, but related, proceedings; in such cases, a multiplicity of proceedings can serve to enhance the administration of justice (see, e.g., Birdseye Security Inc. v. Milosevic, 2020 ONCA 355, at paras. 20-22). The inverse can also be true: pleadings do not need to be identical in order for a multiplicity of proceedings to amount to abuse of process (see, e.g., Dixon, at para. 85; Fillion v. Degen, 2005 MBCA 58, 195 Man. R. (2d) 2, at para. 23).
[40] Thus, the abuse of process analysis does not end when multiple or similar proceedings exist. Rather, the analysis needs to focus on whether allowing the litigation to proceed would violate such principles as judicial economy, consistency, finality and the integrity of the administration of justice, as discussed above. Where, for example, having duplicative proceedings would waste the resources of the parties, courts and witnesses, or risk inconsistent results and therefore undermine the credibility of the judicial process, this can amount to an abuse of process.
[110] In the context of an application to strike pleadings, abuse of process is often wielded in a manner that would not necessarily be intuitively clear to a self‑represented party. The context just cited, where a plaintiff files multiple duplicative or overlapping claims against the same party based on identical or similar facts, provides a such an example: see CSASPP at paras 163-173.
[111] In the present case, abuse of process is far from esoteric or notional. As set out above in the background, the plaintiff has already overtly stated that he intended to use litigation as a mechanism for vengeance and embarrassment and leverage against colleagues and administrators at the Health Authority. The Health Authority's affidavit sets out a series of aggressive correspondence in which the plaintiff threatens and demands payments from Health Authority officials:
a) On July 13, 2023, the plaintiff wrote directly to the Medical Director at the Health Authority, in which he argues the merits of his case and threatens to sue her personally. He demanded $200,000 payment.
b) On July 17, 2023, the plaintiff wrote directly to the Senior Operating Officer of the Health Authority. In that correspondence, the plaintiff stated that, "I fully intend to take you personally to court over what you did in 2018." He demanded $50,000 payment.
c) On July 18, 2023, the plaintiff wrote directly to the Medical Director of the Health Authority. In that correspondence, he stated that, "I reserve the right to pursue all available legal remedies to seek appropriate justice and compensation for the damages incurred. This may include initiating legal proceedings and engaging the media to expose these issues. As you know, I have shown tremendous restraint so far on this matter. You will, however, not get such an opportunity to protect your reputation again." He demanded $50,000 payment.
d) On July 20, 2023, the plaintiff wrote to the CEO of the Health Authority. In that communication, he made allegations similar to those in the proposed claim. He stated that, "failure to address my demands satisfactorily will leave me with no choice but to escalate this matter to the Supreme Court. Such a course of action may severely affect your reputation and financial standing. . . . I am prepared to utilize all available legal resources to ensure justice prevails." He demanded $50,000 payment.
[112] These communications are exacerbated by the fact that the plaintiff well knew at the time that the Health Authority was represented by counsel and that such communications should have been made lawyer-to-lawyer. I acknowledge that the plaintiff is not under the same Law Society rules that require such communications to represented individuals. Nonetheless, as a sophisticated individual, he made these communications, that ought to have been made not directly to those individuals but to the Health Authority's lawyers. I also agree that threatening litigation against these individuals in their personal capacity for their official actions performed on behalf of the Health Authority was inappropriate, especially in light of the expiry of the limitation period.
[113] Further, the present claim, in its original form as well as in the proposed pleadings, is to some extent duplicative of the plaintiff's complaint filed with the BC Human Rights Tribunal against the Health Authority on December 7, 2018. The complaint raises many of the same allegations based upon many of the same underlying facts.
[114] Apart from the abuse of process represented by those duplicative proceedings, the plaintiff in those protracted proceedings exhibited his contempt for that quasi‑judicial body and its members. As set out in the HRT Decision[3] indexed as Dr. A v. Health Authority and another (No. 3), 2023 BCHRT 10, ordering that Dr Afridi pay costs for "extreme, repeat, and deliberate" improper conduct in those proceedings, including non-disclosure, and repeated directions against "sending highly offensive and inappropriate communications notwithstanding multiple warnings":
15 On June 21, 2022, in response to the Disclosure Decision, Dr. A wrote to the Tribunal expressing his disagreement with it. In his email to the Tribunal, Dr. A referred to the Member as "this stupid woman" and described her as "clueless." With respect to the Tribunal, Dr. A wrote, "You guys should be publicly humiliated and laughed at for running this mockery of Justice called the Human Rights Tribunal of BC."
16 The same day, the Tribunal reminded Dr. A about his obligation to copy communications to the other parties. In response, he wrote that he refused to participate "in this mockery of justice" and used profanity, including "Fuck the other parties and fuck this tribunal."
...
18 In response, Dr. A wrote that the Tribunal is "a joke" and he has "nothing but contempt for it." He also addressed the Member, stating, "Madam you should have some shame and resign from this case. You are not fit to pass judgement on it."
...
24 In the letter, the Member also commented on Dr. A's conduct during the case conference. The Member noted that Dr. A interrupted the submissions of the other parties and was rude and disrespectful

[116] The present claim also arises from some, if not most, of the same base facts, and thus overlaps with the plaintiff's Human Rights Tribunal complaint against the College, with respect to its investigation of his complaints of racism and other complaints. In order to avoid a perceived conflict of interest, the College referred the review of that matter to its Alberta counterpart. Dr Afridi filed a further complaint against the Alberta College alleging delay and racism against that body. In reasons indexed as Afridi v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of Alberta, 2024 AHRC 17, the Commission member upheld its Director's earlier decision to dismiss the complaint.
[117] Parenthetically, and also speaking to abuse of process and vexatious litigation behaviour, the plaintiff has also launched Human Rights Tribunal proceedings in Ontario, which appear to level the same sorts of accusations of racism against the Canadian Medical Protective Association, after the plaintiff left British Columbia. As noted in Afridi v. Canadian Medical Protective Association, 2022 HRTO 672, those proceedings spanned over two years: para 4. On the eve of the summary hearing of that matter, the plaintiff himself applied to withdraw his application. As set out in the cited reasons at para 13, the Ontario Human Rights member granted permission for the applicant to withdraw his application at zero hour, but with the following conditions designed to combat abusive proceedings:
a. The applicant is barred from filing any future Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario application against the respondent relating to the incidents or allegations raised in this Application.
b. If the applicant does file such a future application relating to the incidents or allegations raised in this Application, the respondent may seek the summary dismissal of it as an abuse of process.
[118] The Health Authority hypothesises that the present proposed pleading, with its many continued deficiencies, does not represent a continued cack-handed inability to draft a proper pleading, but rather betrays an intentional strategy to continue to attempt to confuse, confound, and cost the defendants. While the Health Authority's theory is intriguing, as with many such conspiracy theories, I fear that it gives too much credit to the plaintiff: Hanlon's Razor. I thus need not opine directly on the Health Authority's theory.
[119] That said, I am satisfied that the plaintiff's multiple claims and complaints, in multiple fora, in multiple provinces, provides further evidence that the present proposed pleading is appropriately struck as a vexatious abuse of process. As noted, there is considerable overlap between the College and the Alberta Human Rights complaints, the BC Human Rights complaints, and the present complaint which, as noted, expressly proposes to plead discrimination and harassment: matters that have already largely been considered by those other adjudicative bodies.
[120] Those complaints, and his conduct during those proceedings, as set out above, also indicate that the plaintiff is contemptuous of adjudicative proceedings. His actions indicate that he is not primarily interested in having his complaints adjudicated by a neutral body. Rather, he is more interested in abusively prosecuting his complaints, and protracting those complaints, for the ulterior purpose of extracting revenge on his former colleagues and supervisors, and the present defendants. The use of adjudicative proceedings for vengeance rather than legitimate adjudication in itself constitutes an abuse of process justifying striking a claim: CFAR at para 52, Babavic v. Babowech, [1993] BCJ No. 1802 at 8-9, 42 ACWS (3d) 447 (SC) ; Johnson v. Smith, 2018 BCSC 836 at paras 49-50; Pearlman v. Critchley, 2012 BCCA 398 at paras 20-22, leave to appeal to SCC ref'd, 35133 (20 March 2013); Grosz v. Jones, 2020 BCSC 1123 at para 74. I am not, however, resting my decision on this principle, as I cannot, on the materials before me, confirm that this ulterior improper purpose was the sole purpose for the proceeding: CFAR at para 52. That said, it provides further indication that dismissal is a just result, in addition to the realisation of Justice Caldwell's order. It also provides further indication that I should exercise my discretion against allowing yet a further amendment.
E. CONCLUSION
[121] I thank counsel for their proficient and comprehensive submissions.
[122] I wish to emphasise that these reasons are not meant as a criticism of Mr Sidhu personally, who served as a skilled advocate in this hearing, and whose name is not on any of the impugned pleadings.
[123] The plaintiff's application is denied. Those of the defendants are granted.
[124] The defendants have been successful in this application and in this action. The plaintiff is presumptively obliged to pay the defendants their costs of this proceeding at Scale B. If any party wishes to dislodge this presumption, that party will advise the others within 15 days of today, and schedule with the Registry a date as soon as reasonably practicable to argue this matter. Each party will provide a written argument to the other parties and to the court at least seven days before the hearing date.
[125] As a final comment with respect to costs, this contemplation of a costs hearing is not an encouragement that we actually have a costs hearing. On one hand, I expect that the defendants will be desirous to argue that it should be entitled to special costs, as the strike provisions in the Rules expressly contemplate, and given the considerable efforts to which this five‑year proceeding and other proceedings referred to in these reasons have put the defendants. On the other hand, I suspect that Dr Afridi will continue his grave umbrage at the actual or perceived actions of the defendants and resist actual payment of any costs.
[126] The strike provisions in the Rules expressly contemplate an award of special costs--that is more or less full compensatory, full indemnity, costs-- in the case of a successful strike application. My reasons have outlined--if only scratching the surface--the remarkable circumstances of this particular hearing and the history of this proceeding and related proceedings. A party in Dr Afridi's shoes would be well advised to reach some sort of reasonable arrangement with respect to costs, and thus avoid a costs hearing.
[127] That all said, I will keep my mind open if and when a costs hearing is necessitated by the actions of one side or the other.
"Crerar J."
